behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is real filesystem watch semantics for nested paths (recursive vs non-recursive behavior), which depends on OS/watcher implementation. In-process mocks can force subdirectory events and hide a real miss.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a new file in a nested subdirectory, and assert one transformed output appears in the output directory without restart.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is about module handoff (request intake -> dedup window state -> pipeline dispatch) and time-window state propagation, not OS-specific watcher behavior. A local in-process setup can expose real failures here.
behaviors.test_description: Wire intake, dedup store/cache, and dispatcher with a controllable clock; submit two requests for the same path within the configured window; assert first is dispatched and second is dropped, then assert dispatch resumes after window expiry.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected System because behavior text is about "processing request" dedup within a time window, not real OS/network/subprocess conditions.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly anchors to OS-emitted duplicate events. Mocking event streams can miss real watcher burst patterns and coalescing differences.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with real watcher on a temp directory; perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple filesystem notifications; verify exactly one pipeline execution/output for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is a real filesystem lifecycle condition (delete/recreate path while watcher is active). Correctness depends on OS-level watch invalidation and re-subscription behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Launch daemon watching a real temp directory, remove the watched directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and assert it is processed without restarting the daemon process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: The ordering key is filesystem creation timestamps under concurrent arrival, which is tied to real FS timestamp behavior and event timing. Pure mocks can hide precision/race issues.
behaviors.test_description: Using a real watched directory, create multiple files nearly simultaneously with controlled timestamp differences (or pre-set metadata where supported), then assert processing order matches observed creation timestamps rather than raw event arrival order.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected Integration because behavior text requires "arrive at the same time" and ordering by filesystem creation timestamps, a real-infrastructure boundary.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This requires real partial-write timing and filesystem event behavior (arrival before close/flush). In-process fakes often model writes atomically and miss the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real directory; open a file and write it in chunks with delays while keeping the handle open; assert no processing/output occurs until writer closes and final content is stable, then assert single successful processing.
